author: bindData
content:
The data-join is the one idea that unlocks D3. You are not telling D3 to draw 10 bars. You hand it an array and describe what a bar looks like for one item.

Think React: you map over data and return JSX. D3 does the same, but splits it into three buckets — enter (new data, no element yet), update (data + element exist), exit (element with no data anymore).
code:
const bars = svg.selectAll("rect").data(values);
bars.enter().append("rect");   // new
bars.attr("height", d => d);    // update
bars.exit().remove();           // gone

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: The phrase that finally clicked for me: selectAll on an empty selection is a promise about elements that should exist, not a query for ones that do.

author: scaleLinear
content:
A scale is just a translator between two number lines.

Your data lives in one world (sales from 0 to 5000). Your screen lives in another (0 to 600 pixels tall). A scale maps one onto the other so you never do the math by hand.

It is the same mental move as CSS calc() turning a percentage into pixels, except D3 gives you the function.
code:
const y = d3.scaleLinear()
  .domain([0, 5000])   // data world
  .range([400, 0]);    // pixel world (flipped: SVG y grows down)
y(2500); // -> 200

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: The flipped range trips up everyone. SVG y=0 is the top, so range goes [height, 0] to make bigger values sit higher.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: Once this clicks, scaleBand for categorical axes feels like the same idea with padding built in.

author: geoProjection
content:
Hot take: most people reach for D3 when they only needed SVG and a loop.

If your chart never updates, you do not need the data-join. Render static `<rect>` elements in React, position them with a scale function, done. D3's superpower is animated transitions between data states. Use it for that, not for drawing five fixed bars.

comments.author: transition_ease
comments.content: Agreed. I import d3-scale and d3-shape for the math and let React own the DOM. Best of both worlds for static dashboards.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: Fair, but the moment your data streams in live, hand-rolling enter/update/exit in React gets old fast.

author: transition_ease
content:
Spot the bug. This bar chart renders fine on first load, but when the data array shrinks, old bars stick around like ghosts.

What is missing?

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: No exit().remove(). When data shrinks, the leftover elements have no bound datum, so they never get cleaned up. Add bars.exit().remove().

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: Also worth using .join("rect") in modern D3 — it handles enter, update, and exit in one call so you stop forgetting this.

author: transition_ease
content:
A D3 transition is just CSS transitions, but driven by JavaScript instead of stylesheets.

You set a duration, then any attr you change animates from its current value to the new one. The difference: D3 can tween SVG attributes CSS cannot touch, like the `d` path string of a line.

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: Add .ease(d3.easeCubicOut) to match the snappy feel of a good CSS cubic-bezier. The defaults are fine but tuning easing is where motion starts feeling intentional.

author: bindData
content:
Common interview question: what is the key function in a data-join and why does it matter?

By default D3 joins data to elements by index. Pass a key function and it joins by identity instead — so when your array reorders, each element follows its own datum instead of swapping values. Same problem React's key prop solves in lists.
code:
svg.selectAll("circle")
  .data(people, d => d.id);  // match by id, not position

comments.author: transition_ease
comments.content: And this is exactly why transitions look right with a key function — an element keeps its identity, so it animates from its old spot to its new spot instead of teleporting values.

author: svgArtist
content:
TIL the SVG coordinate system has y growing downward, just like CSS absolute positioning with top.

That is why a bar chart's y scale range is usually [height, 0] and not [0, height]. You are flipping the axis so larger data values map to smaller y pixels, which visually sit higher. It is the same gotcha as position: absolute; top: 0 being the top edge.
code:
const y = d3.scaleLinear().domain([0, max]).range([height, 0]);
// rect y = y(value), rect height = height - y(value)

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: The height = baseline minus y(value) line is the other half people miss. The rect grows down from its top edge, so you compute both y and height.

author: geoProjection
content:
A map projection is just a scale for two dimensions at once.

A linear scale turns one number into one pixel. A projection turns a [longitude, latitude] pair into an [x, y] pixel pair, while bending the math so a sphere fits on a flat screen. Same input-to-output translator role, harder geometry under the hood.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: And geoPath is the data-join's append step for maps — it turns each GeoJSON feature into the d attribute of a path. Projection does the where, geoPath does the draw.

author: svgArtist
content:
How a D3 line chart actually gets drawn, in three steps. A thread.

1. Scales map your data to pixels (x for time, y for value).
2. d3.line() reads those scales and builds one long SVG path string — the d attribute.
3. You append a single `<path>` and set its d. One element, not one per point.

That last part surprises people coming from bar charts.

comments.author: transition_ease
comments.content: Because the whole line is one path string, animating it means tweening that string. That is the part CSS transitions literally cannot do, which is why D3 owns line animation.

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: d3.line() and d3.geoPath() are cousins — both are path generators that turn data into a d attribute. Learn one and the other feels familiar.

author: scaleLinear
content:
Using D3 inside React? Let each library do what it is best at.

React owns the DOM and re-renders. D3 owns the math. Import d3-scale and d3-shape for scales and path generators, then return plain JSX. Skip selectAll and append entirely — those fight React for control of the same nodes.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: This is the pattern I default to now. Use a ref + raw D3 only when you need transitions React cannot express cleanly, like a tweened path morph.

comments.author: geoProjection
comments.content: Same applies to maps: compute the projection and run features through geoPath, then render the resulting strings as JSX path elements. React stays the owner.
